text: The surgeon begins a left-sided colon resection, encountering complex anatomy beneath the transverse colon mesentery. The transverse colon appears adhered to the descending colon, complicating identification of anatomical structures. The surgeon performs lateral dissection around the splenic flexure to clarify the anatomical layout.

The surgeon identifies the transverse colon and the omentum on the distal transverse colon, proceeding to dissect the omentum to mobilize the splenic flexure. The surgeon performs lateral dissection to medialize the bowel due to anatomical confusion preventing the planned sub-IMV dissection.

The surgeon unfolds the transverse colon and descending colon to better identify the mesentery, revealing a tattoo in the proximal descending colon. The transverse colon is attached to the descending colon, and the surgeon attempts to separate and unfold these structures to clarify the anatomy. Beneath the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), a hernia defect is visible, with most of the small bowel located above the splenic flexure in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon observes a large defect without signs of bowel obstruction or abdominal pain. The transverse colon is separated from the descending colon, revealing congenital attachments. The surgeon aims to preserve as much bowel as possible while reducing the structures around the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon identifies a defect beneath the mesentery and proceeds to take down lateral attachments to straighten the bowel. The colon mesentery is placed under medial tension, and dissection continues over the kidney. The left ureter is visible in the distance, facilitated by the patient's thin physique.

The surgeon removes the last lateral attachments of the descending colon leading up to the splenic flexure. The ligament of Treitz is visible laterally, and the omentum is seen at the distal transverse colon. The surgeon further mobilizes the colon laterally to relieve tension from the attachments, despite the bowel's redundancy. The surgeon identifies the distal margin for resection, noting the tumor's location in the proximal descending colon, indicating a splenic flexure resection.

The surgeon addresses robust lateral adhesions of the sigmoid colon to the pelvic sidewall, opting for a lateral to medial approach to mobilize the colon. The sigmoid is prepared for mesenteric transaction, with proximal and distal margins being planned.

The surgeon plans to take the mesentery to the proximal colon first, holding the proximal margin with the left hand while dissecting the mesentery along the sub IMV region. The thick tissues are visible, likely due to chronic friction from bowel herniation beneath, and the blood vessels are not extensively dissected.

The surgeon dissects the left colic artery from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) along the aorta, aiming to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal artery. The dissection reveals the superior hemorrhoidal artery descending and the left colic artery positioned superiorly. Sigmoidal branches are visible coming off the IMA. The surgeon prepares to use a vessel sealer to complete the dissection.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the left colic branch and traces it up to the chosen distal margin in the mid sigmoid colon. The camera is repositioned to facilitate the procedure, with a twelve millimeter trocar placed as arm number three for optimal access. The port closure is performed through the rectus to minimize hernia risk and reduce pain at the closure site.

The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis, joining two staple lines with 2-0 PDS sutures. The camera is positioned at port number three, allowing the surgeon to work with two left hands and one right hand. The colotomies are made, and the ICG fluorescence is checked, confirming good perfusion.

The surgeon performs the inner layer of the anastomosis using three OV lock sutures, starting in the middle and running towards the camera. The two sutures are brought together in a canal fashion and tied, completing the outer anterior row with two OPDS sutures. This results in a two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon plans a natural orifice extraction of the specimen via a transvaginal route. An EEA sizer is placed into the vagina and held by the nurse. A vaginotomy is performed, followed by the introduction of a 15mm endocatch bag. The cancerous specimen is placed in the bag, which is then closed and removed. The bag is opened outside, allowing the bowel to unfold and be extracted easily. The vaginotomy appears small with minimal tearing.
The surgeon closes the surgical site in two layers, starting with the mucosa and muscular layer, followed by closing the peritoneum over the top.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical field, observing the mobilized splenic flexure and the ligament of Treitz. A leak test is conducted on the anastomosis near the flexure, confirming no bubbles are present, indicating a secure closure.
